The **Feast of the Ascension of Jesus Christ**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (also called the **Solemnity of the Ascension of the Lord**, **Ascension Day**, **Ascension Thursday**; or sometimes **Holy Thursday**, which can also mean [the Thursday before Easter](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"))[[2]](#cite_note-Forster-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Soane-3) commemorates the Christian belief of the bodily [Ascension of Jesus](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") into Heaven. It is one of the [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") (shared by multiple [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination")) feasts of Christian churches, ranking with the feasts of the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"). Following the account of [Acts 1:3](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Acts%201:3&version=nrsv) that the risen Jesus appeared for 40 days prior to his Ascension, Ascension Day is traditionally celebrated on a Thursday, the fortieth day of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") according to [inclusive counting](/wiki/Inclusive_counting "Inclusive counting"), although some Christian denominations have moved the observance to the following Sunday, sometimes called **Ascension Sunday**. The day of observance varies by ecclesiastical province in many Christian denominations, as with Lutherans and Catholics, for example.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Ascensiontide** refers to the ten-day period between the Feast of the Ascension and the Feast of Pentecost.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Sunday within that period may be referred to as the Seventh Sunday of Easter or the Sunday in Ascensiontide.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The observance of this feast is of great antiquity. [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius "Eusebius") seems to hint at the celebration of it in the 4th century.[[7]](#cite_note-7) At the beginning of the 5th century, [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") says that it is of [Apostolic](/wiki/Apostolic_Fathers "Apostolic Fathers") origin, and he speaks of it in a way that shows it was the universal observance of the Catholic Church long before his time. Frequent mention of it is made in the writings of [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom"), [Gregory of Nyssa](/wiki/Gregory_of_Nyssa "Gregory of Nyssa"), and in the *[Constitution of the Apostles](/wiki/Apostolic_Constitutions "Apostolic Constitutions")*. The *[Pilgrimage of Aetheria](/wiki/Egeria_(pilgrim) "Egeria (pilgrim)")* speaks of the vigil of this feast and of the feast itself, as they were kept in the church built over the grotto in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem") in which Christ is traditionally regarded as having been born.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It may be that prior to the 5th century the event narrated in the Gospels was commemorated in conjunction with the feast of Easter or Pentecost. Some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] believe that the much-disputed forty-third decree of the [Synod of Elvira](/wiki/Synod_of_Elvira "Synod of Elvira") (c. 300) condemning the practice of observing a feast on the fortieth day after Easter and neglecting to keep Pentecost on the fiftieth day, implies that the proper usage of the time was to commemorate the Ascension along with Pentecost.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Representations of the [mystery](/wiki/Sacred_mysteries#Christian_mysteries "Sacred mysteries") are found in [diptychs](/wiki/Diptych "Diptych") and [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") dating as early as the 5th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Hymns for this feast are found in the Georgian Chantbook of Jerusalem,[[9]](#cite_note-9) which contains material composed during the 5th century.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## In Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: In Western Christianity")]

The Latin terms used for the feast, *ascensio* and, occasionally, *ascensa*, signify that Christ was raised up by his own powers, and it is from these terms that the holy day gets its name. In the [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer") of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), "Holy Thursday" is listed as another name for Ascension Day.[[2]](#cite_note-Forster-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Soane-3)[[11]](#cite_note-11) [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake")'s poem "[Holy Thursday](/wiki/Holy_Thursday_(Songs_of_Innocence) "Holy Thursday (Songs of Innocence)")" refers to Ascension Day; Thomas Pruen used the term to refer to Ascension Day in his *Illustration of the Liturgy of the Church of England*, published in 1820;[[12]](#cite_note-Pruen1820-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Keene2000-13) however use of the term "Holy Thursday" to mean Ascension Day is rare,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and the term is more generally applied by most Christian denominations to [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") in [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").

In [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), the earliest possible date is April 30 (as in 1818 and 2285), the latest possible date is June 3 (as in 1943 and 2038). In the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the Ascension of the Lord is ranked as a [Solemnity](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity") and is a [Holy Day of Obligation](/wiki/Holy_Day_of_Obligation "Holy Day of Obligation"). In the [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), the Feast of the Ascension is a feast.[[15]](#cite_note-EinertsonMuench-15) In the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), Ascension Day is a [Principal Feast](/wiki/Principal_Feast "Principal Feast").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The three days before Ascension Thursday are sometimes referred to as the [Rogation days](/wiki/Rogation_days "Rogation days"), and the previous Sunday—the Sixth Sunday of Easter (or the Fifth Sunday *after* Easter)—as [Rogation Sunday](/wiki/Rogation_Sunday "Rogation Sunday").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ascension has a [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil") and, since the 15th century, an [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)"), which is set apart for a [novena](/wiki/Novena "Novena") of preparation for Pentecost.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In the Book of Common Prayer of the Church of England the Sunday following Ascension Day is called the Sunday after the Ascension.[[17]](#cite_note-17) For Anglo-Catholics Ascension Day is a double of the 1st class with a privileged octave of the third order.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In traditional Methodist usage, *[The Book of Worship for Church and Home](/wiki/Book_of_Worship_for_Church_and_Home_(1965) "Book of Worship for Church and Home (1965)")* (1965) provides the following [Collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") for Ascension Day, commonly called Holy Thursday:[[19]](#cite_note-BOW1964-19)

> Almighty God, whose blessed Son our Saviour Jesus Christ ascended far above all heavens, that he might fill all things: Mercifully give us faith to perceive that according to his promise he abideth with his Church on earth, even unto the end of the world; through the same thy Son Jesus Christ our Lord. **Amen.**[[19]](#cite_note-BOW1964-19)

Catholic parishes in a number of countries that do not observe the feast as a public holiday have obtained permission from the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") to move observance of the Feast of the Ascension from the traditional Thursday to the following Sunday, the Sunday before Pentecost. Similarly, the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") allows the traditional celebration on Holy Thursday to be moved to Sunday.[[20]](#cite_note-Hickman2011-20) This is in keeping with a trend to move [Holy Days of Obligation](/wiki/Holy_Day_of_Obligation "Holy Day of Obligation") from weekdays to Sunday, to encourage more Christians to observe feasts considered important.[[21]](#cite_note-stcloud-21)[[22]](#cite_note-sun-22) The decision to move a feast is made by each [Conference of Catholic Bishops](/wiki/Episcopal_conference "Episcopal conference") with prior approval of the [Apostolic See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See").[[23]](#cite_note-23) In some cases the Conference may delegate the determination for specific feasts to the bishops of an [ecclesiastical province](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") within the conference, i.e. an [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") and the neighbouring bishops.

The switch to Sunday was made in 1992 by the [Conference of Catholic Bishops in Australia](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Australia "Roman Catholicism in Australia");[[24]](#cite_note-24) before 1996 in parts of Europe;[[25]](#cite_note-25) in 1997 in [Ireland](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Ireland "Roman Catholicism in Ireland");[[26]](#cite_note-26) before 1998 [in States](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Roman Catholicism in the United States") from 1999;[[21]](#cite_note-stcloud-21) and in [England and Chair of Saint Peter"). When celebrated on a Sunday, the earliest possible date is May 3, and the latest is June 6.

## In Eastern and Oriental Orthodox Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In Eastern and Oriental Orthodox Christianity")]

In the [Eastern Church](/wiki/Eastern_Church "Eastern Church") this feast is known in Greek as *Analepsis*, the "taking up", and also as the *Episozomene*, the "salvation from on high", denoting that by ascending into his glory Christ completed the work of our [redemption](/wiki/Redemption_(theology) "Redemption (theology)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Ascension is one of the Twelve [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts") of the Orthodox [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ascension2007-04.jpg/250px-Ascension2007-04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ascension2007-04.jpg)

Liturgy during the Feast of the Ascension in a Mumbai [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church_in_India "Syriac Orthodox Church in India")

### Celebration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Celebration")]

The feast is always observed with an [All-night vigil](/wiki/All-night_vigil "All-night vigil"). The day before is the [Apodosis](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") (leave-taking) of Easter (i.e., the last day of the Feast of Easter). Before the Vigil, the [Paschal Hours](/wiki/Paschal_Hours "Paschal Hours") are said for the last time and the [Paschal greeting](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting") is exchanged.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The *Paroemia* ([Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") readings) at [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") on the eve of the Feast are [Isaiah 2:2–3](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Isaiah#2:2 "s:Bible (King James)/Isaiah"); [Isaiah 62:10–63:3](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Isaiah#62:10 "s:Bible (King James)/Isaiah"), [63:7–9](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Isaiah#63:7 "s:Bible (King James)/Isaiah"); and [Zechariah 14:1–4](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Zechariah#14:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Zechariah"), [14:8–11](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Zechariah#14:8 "s:Bible (King James)/Zechariah").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A [Lity](/wiki/Lity_(Orthodox_Vespers) "Lity (Orthodox Vespers)") is celebrated. The [troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion") of the day is sung, which says:[*[This quote needs a citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Inline_citation#When_you_must_use_inline_citations "Wikipedia:Inline citation")*]

> O Christ God, You have ascended in Glory,  
> Granting joy to Your disciples by the promise of the Holy Spirit.  
> Through the blessing they were assured  
> That You are the Son of God,  
> The Redeemer of the world!

During the [Polyeleos](/wiki/Polyeleos "Polyeleos") at [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins"), the [Epitaphios](/wiki/Epitaphios_(liturgical) "Epitaphios (liturgical)"), which was placed on the altar on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") (either at Matins or the [Midnight Office](/wiki/Midnight_Office "Midnight Office"), depending on local custom) is taken from the altar and carried in procession around the church. It is then put in the place reserved for it. The Gospel is [Mark 16:9–20](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Mark#16:9 "s:Bible (King James)/Mark"). The kontakion is sung, which announces:[*[This quote needs a citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Inline_citation#When_you_must_use_inline_citations "Wikipedia:Inline citation")*]

> When You did fulfill the dispensation for our sake,  
> And unite earth to Heaven:  
> You did ascend in glory, O Christ our God,  
> Not being parted from those who love You,  
> But remaining with them and crying:  
> I am with you and no one will be against you.

The [megalynarion](/wiki/Megalynarion "Megalynarion") and [irmos](/wiki/Irmos "Irmos") from Ode IX of the Canon (also sung at liturgy) is:

> Magnify, O my soul, Christ the Giver of Life,  
> Who has ascended from earth to heaven!  
> We magnify you, the Mother of God,  
> Who beyond reason and understanding  
> gave birth in time to the Timeless One.

At the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy"), special antiphons are sung in place of Psalms 102 and 145 and the Beatitudes. The [Epistle](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") is [Acts 1:1–12](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Acts#1:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Acts"), and the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") is [Luke 24:36–53](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#24:36 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Observance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Observance")]

Ascension Thursday also commemorates the Holy [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church") [Martyrs](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") of Persia (17th–18th centuries).[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Ascension has an [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") of eight days. The Sunday after Ascension is the Sunday of the [Holy Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") of the [First Ecumenical Council](/wiki/First_Ecumenical_Council "First Ecumenical Council") at [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea "Nicaea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This council formulated the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") up to the words, "He (Jesus) ascended into heaven, and sits at the right hand of the Father; and shall come again, with glory, to judge the living and the dead; Whose kingdom shall have no end."[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Afterfeast ends on the following Friday, the Friday before Pentecost. The next day is appropriately a [Saturday of the Dead](/wiki/All_Souls_Day "All Souls Day") (general commemoration of all faithful departed).

The Eastern Orthodox Church uses a different method of [calculating](/wiki/Computus "Computus") the [date of Easter](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter"), so the Eastern Orthodox commemoration of Ascension will usually be after the western observance (either one week, or four weeks, or five weeks later; but occasionally on the same day). The earliest possible date for the feast is May 13 (of the Western calendar), and the latest possible date is June 16. Some of the Oriental Orthodox Churches, however, observe Ascension on the same date as the Western Churches.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Feast_of_the_Ascension&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Music")]

The [feast has been associated](/wiki/List_of_church_cantatas_by_liturgical_occasion#Ascension "List of church cantatas by liturgical occasion") with specific hymns and other church music. The oldest [hymn](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") in German related to the feast is the [Leise](/wiki/Leise "Leise") "[Christ fuhr gen Himmel](/wiki/Christ_fuhr_gen_Himmel "Christ fuhr gen Himmel")", first published in 1480. [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") composed several [cantatas](/wiki/Bach_cantata "Bach cantata") and the *[Ascension Oratorio](/wiki/Ascension_Oratorio "Ascension Oratorio")* to be performed in church services on the feast day. He first performed *[Wer da gläubet und getauft wird, BWV 37](/wiki/Wer_da_gl%C3%A4ubet_und_getauft_wird,_BWV_37 "Wer da gläubet und getauft wird, BWV 37")*, on 18 May 1724, *[Auf Christi Himmelfahrt allein, BWV 128](/wiki/Auf_Christi_Himmelfahrt_allein,_BWV_128 "Auf Christi Himmelfahrt allein, BWV 128")*, on 10 May 1725, *[Gott fähret auf mit Jauchzen, BWV 43](/wiki/Gott_f%C3%A4hret_auf_mit_Jauchzen,_BWV_43 "Gott fähret auf mit Jauchzen, BWV 43")*, on 30 May 1726 and the oratorio, *[Lobet Gott in seinen Reichen, BWV 11](/wiki/Lobet_Gott_in_seinen_Reichen,_BWV_11 "Lobet Gott in seinen Reichen, BWV 11")*, on 19 May 1735.

Many [Messianic psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") are used at the feast of Ascension including [Psalm 24](/wiki/Psalm_24 "Psalm 24"), [Psalm 47](/wiki/Psalm_47 "Psalm 47") and [Psalm 68](/wiki/Psalm_68 "Psalm 68"). The dialogue "Lift up your heads, O ye gates" from Psalm 24 (verses 7–10) has inspired Handel's setting in Part II of his *[Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)")* in the scene "[Ascension](/wiki/Messiah_Part_II#33 "Messiah Part II")", and [Christoph Bernhard Verspoell](/wiki/Christoph_Bernhard_Verspoell "Christoph Bernhard Verspoell")'s 1810 hymn in German, "[Öffnet eure Tore](/wiki/%C3%96ffnet_eure_Tore "Öffnet eure Tore")". [Phillip Moore](/wiki/Philip_Moore_(organist) "Philip Moore (organist)")'s anthem *The Ascension* sets words based on the same verses.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[Olivier Messiaen](/wiki/Olivier_Messiaen "Olivier Messiaen") wrote an orchestral suite, later partly transcribed for organ, called *[L'Ascension](/wiki/L%27Ascension "L'Ascension")* in the 1930s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Settings of "God is gone up" have been composed by [William Croft](/wiki/William_Croft "William Croft"), [Arthur Hutchings](/wiki/Arthur_Hutchings "Arthur Hutchings") and [Gerald Finzi](/wiki/Gerald_Finzi "Gerald Finzi") (words by Edward Taylor).[[35]](#cite_note-35) Other settings suitable for the occasion include [William Matthias](/wiki/William_Mathias "William Mathias")'s *Lift up your heads*. "[Im Himmel hoch verherrlicht ist](/wiki/Im_Himmel_hoch_verherrlicht_ist "Im Himmel hoch verherrlicht ist")" (Highly gloryfied in Heaven) is a 1973 hymn in German for the occasion.

The [RSCM](/wiki/Royal_School_of_Church_Music "Royal School of Church Music") has produced an extensive list of music (including hymns, anthems and organ music) suitable for Ascension.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
